claimText: Transposable elements, or jumping genes, are DNA sequences that can move from one location to another within the genome.
evidenceExcerpt: Jumping genes (transposable elements) are DNA sequences that move from one location to another on the genome.
examUse: Statement-based question on the definition of jumping genes.
distractorHooks: RNA sequences that exit the nucleus
Extrachromosomal plasmids found only in bacteria

claimText: More than 50 percent of the human genome is composed of transposable elements.
evidenceExcerpt: More than 50% of the human genome is composed of transposable elements.
examUse: Fact-based statement in a multi-statement question regarding human genome composition.
distractorHooks: Less than 2 percent of the human genome
Only found in plant genomes, not humans
Make up exactly 10 percent of the human genome

claimText: Jumping genes move across the genome by either physically cutting and pasting or copying themselves to new locations.
evidenceExcerpt: They move by either physically cutting and pasting or copying themselves to new locations.
examUse: Statement-based question on the mechanism of transposable elements.
distractorHooks: Move by transforming into RNA permanently

claimText: LINE-1 is a jumping gene that binds to cellular DNA during brief periods of cell division.
evidenceExcerpt: LINE-1 is a jumping gene that binds to cellular DNA during brief periods of cell division.
examUse: Correct-pair question matching LINE-1 to its biological function or classification.
distractorHooks: LINE-1 is a synthetic cloning vector
LINE-1 binds to DNA during apoptosis

claimText: The movement of transposable elements can affect gene expression and cause mutations in the genome.
evidenceExcerpt: affecting gene expression by cutting and pasting or copying themselves. They can cause mutations
examUse: Statement-based question on the genetic impact of jumping genes.
distractorHooks: Jumping genes stabilize the genome and prevent mutations
Jumping genes have no impact on gene expression
Jumping genes only repair damaged DNA
